name: Recruitment Channel Optimization
decision_axis: This lever controls recruitment speed and sample diversity by choosing between hospital-based referrals, community outreach, and digital pre-screening.
consequences: Pulling this lever accelerates enrollment when hospital referrals dominate, but may bias the cohort toward patients already engaged with tertiary care. Community outreach expands reach to underserved populations, yet increases logistical overhead and screening burden. Digital pre-screening offers rapid triage and cost savings, yet risks lower data quality if participants self-select without clinical confirmation. Each option trades off speed, representativeness, and resource intensity.
options: Recruit participants through hospital-based referrals, leveraging existing clinical pathways to streamline enrollment and reduce screening time.
Engage community outreach programs, partnering with local sleep clinics and patient advocacy groups to broaden demographic representation and increase enrollment volume.
Implement digital pre-screening, deploying an online questionnaire and automated eligibility filter to identify potential participants before in-person visits.
review: The tension lies between enrollment speed and cohort representativeness; the options overlook the impact of screening accuracy on downstream data quality and the potential for digital pre-screening to miss subtle clinical nuances.

name: Staffing Shift Structure
decision_axis: This lever controls staffing fatigue and coverage efficiency by choosing between 8‑hour shifts, 12‑hour shifts, and flexible shift rotation.
consequences: Choosing 8‑hour shifts maximizes technician alertness but increases staffing costs and administrative complexity. Opting for 12‑hour shifts reduces payroll overhead but heightens fatigue risk and may compromise event response quality. Flexible shift rotation offers a balance, allowing technicians to alternate shift lengths and maintain coverage while mitigating burnout, yet requires sophisticated scheduling systems and may complicate training consistency.
options: Adopt 8‑hour shifts, scheduling technicians in two consecutive blocks to maintain high alertness during peak event monitoring periods.
Implement 12‑hour shifts, rotating technicians weekly to reduce payroll expenses while ensuring continuous coverage across all suites.
Introduce flexible shift rotation, allowing technicians to alternate between 8‑hour and 12‑hour blocks based on workload forecasts and personal fatigue metrics.
review: The core tension is balancing technician well‑being against operational cost; the options fail to address how shift patterns influence inter‑rater reliability during annotation and the potential need for cross‑training across shift types.

name: Data Annotation Workflow
decision_axis: This lever controls annotation throughput and data quality by choosing between real‑time annotation, batch annotation, and hybrid annotation.
consequences: Real‑time annotation ensures immediate feedback and reduces backlog, but demands high technician availability and may compromise accuracy under fatigue. Batch annotation allows technicians to focus on a larger dataset at once, improving consistency, yet delays data availability for analysis. Hybrid annotation combines initial real‑time flagging with subsequent batch refinement, potentially balancing speed and precision but requiring additional coordination and software support.
options: Perform real‑time annotation, having technicians label events immediately during each night to capture context and reduce post‑hoc error.
Adopt batch annotation, assigning technicians to review and score entire nights after data transfer, enabling systematic quality checks and inter‑rater calibration.
Implement hybrid annotation, flagging probable events in real‑time and refining them in batch sessions to combine speed with thoroughness.
review: The tension is between speed and accuracy; the options overlook how annotation workflow choices affect inter‑rater reliability and the need for automated quality assurance checks.

name: Event Triage Algorithm Development
decision_axis: This lever controls diagnostic efficiency by choosing between rule‑based heuristics, machine learning classifiers, and hybrid rule‑plus‑ML models.
consequences: Rule‑based heuristics provide transparent, low‑resource solutions but may miss subtle event patterns, limiting sensitivity. Machine learning classifiers can capture complex signatures, improving detection rates, yet require large labeled datasets and risk overfitting. Hybrid rule‑plus‑ML models blend domain knowledge with data‑driven insights, potentially achieving higher accuracy while maintaining interpretability, but increase development complexity and validation burden.
options: Develop rule‑based heuristics, encoding expert sleep‑medicine criteria into threshold‑based algorithms to flag likely parasomnia events.
Train machine learning classifiers, feeding annotated PSG and sensor data into supervised models to learn complex event signatures.
Combine rule‑based and machine learning approaches, applying heuristic filters before feeding data into classifiers to reduce false positives and improve interpretability.
review: The core tension is balancing interpretability against detection performance; the options ignore the need for continuous model retraining as new event types emerge during the longitudinal study.

name: Participant Compensation Model
decision_axis: This lever controls recruitment motivation and retention by choosing between flat per‑night fee, tiered compensation, and incentive‑based bonus.
consequences: Flat per‑night fee offers predictable budgeting and straightforward communication, but may not sufficiently motivate participants with high travel costs. Tiered compensation rewards longer stays, encouraging data completeness, yet complicates payment schedules and may create inequity among participants. Incentive‑based bonuses tied to event capture or study milestones can boost engagement and data yield, but risk introducing selection bias and require careful monitoring to avoid coercion.
options: Offer a flat per‑night fee, paying participants €80 each night regardless of event occurrence to simplify budgeting and communication.
Implement tiered compensation, increasing nightly payment for each consecutive week of stay to incentivize longer participation and richer data capture.
Introduce incentive‑based bonuses, awarding participants a bonus for each confirmed parasomnia event they contribute to the dataset to encourage active engagement.
review: The tension is between motivating participants and maintaining data integrity; the options fail to consider how compensation structures might influence event reporting accuracy or introduce differential dropout rates.

name: Data Sharing Policy
decision_axis: This lever controls data accessibility and privacy compliance by choosing between restricted de‑identified sharing, open repository with controlled access, and embargoed release.
consequences: Restricted de‑identified sharing limits external reuse but ensures strict privacy compliance and aligns with current ethics approvals. Open repository with controlled access accelerates scientific collaboration and citation potential, yet requires robust data governance and monitoring of downstream use. Embargoed release postpones public availability until after primary publication, protecting competitive advantage but delaying broader community benefit and potentially reducing transparency.
options: Maintain restricted de‑identified sharing, providing only anonymized physiological data to vetted researchers under signed agreements.
Publish data in an open repository with controlled access, allowing external scientists to request datasets after completing a data‑use agreement.
Adopt an embargoed release, withholding full dataset until after the first set of peer‑reviewed publications to safeguard intellectual property.
review: The tension lies between privacy protection and scientific openness; the options overlook how embargoed release may delay external validation and the need for a clear data‑governance framework to manage controlled access.

name: Sleep Suite Environmental Calibration
decision_axis: This lever controls environmental noise reduction by choosing between acoustic paneling, active noise cancellation, and adaptive ventilation.
consequences: Implementing acoustic paneling reduces ambient noise, improving signal quality for EEG recordings, but increases renovation cost and may alter room aesthetics. Active noise cancellation offers real‑time noise suppression, potentially enhancing data fidelity, yet requires continuous power and maintenance. Adaptive ventilation controls airflow to reduce humidity and mold risk, supporting participant comfort, but may introduce airflow noise that could interfere with sensor data.
options: Install acoustic paneling throughout all suites to dampen external and internal noise while preserving a domestic feel.
Deploy active noise cancellation units in each suite to suppress ambient sound during recording sessions.
Configure adaptive ventilation systems to maintain optimal humidity while minimizing airflow noise that could interfere with sensor data.
review: Balancing acoustic quality against aesthetic integrity and cost; options overlook the impact of paneling on naturalistic behavior and the need for routine maintenance of active systems.

name: Participant Engagement Protocol
decision_axis: This lever controls participant adherence by choosing between structured pre‑stay orientation, real‑time feedback loops, and post‑stay debriefing.
consequences: Providing a structured orientation improves participant understanding of procedures, potentially reducing drop‑outs, but adds scheduling overhead. Real‑time feedback loops, such as nightly summaries, may increase engagement and data quality, yet require additional staff time. Post‑stay debriefing can capture participant insights for protocol refinement, but may delay data analysis if not integrated efficiently.
options: Offer a structured pre‑stay orientation session that explains monitoring equipment and daily routines and expectations.
Implement nightly real‑time feedback loops that summarize sleep metrics and encourage participants to adjust habits.
Conduct post‑stay debriefing interviews to gather participant experiences and identify protocol improvements for future studies.
review: Balancing engagement depth with resource constraints; options miss the trade‑off between participant burden and data richness.

name: Ethics Oversight Cadence
decision_axis: This lever controls ethics review frequency by choosing between quarterly, bi‑annual, and event‑driven reviews.
consequences: Quarterly reviews ensure rapid response to emerging issues, but may strain board capacity. Bi‑annual reviews reduce administrative load, yet risk delayed detection of compliance gaps. Event‑driven reviews trigger only after incidents, minimizing routine burden but potentially allowing problems to accumulate.
options: Schedule ethics board reviews every quarter to promptly address emerging participant concerns and safety protocols.
Conduct ethics board reviews twice a year to balance oversight with administrative efficiency and compliance.
Trigger ethics board reviews only after documented incidents to reduce routine oversight and ensure accountability.
review: Tension between oversight rigor and administrative burden; options overlook the need for continuous monitoring of data security practices.

name: Data Retention and Archival Strategy
decision_axis: This lever controls data storage duration by choosing between 5‑year, 10‑year, and indefinite retention.
consequences: A 5‑year retention aligns with typical grant reporting, reducing storage costs but limiting long‑term reuse. A 10‑year retention supports extended research but increases storage and security obligations. Indefinite retention preserves all data for future studies but requires ongoing compliance with evolving privacy regulations.
options: Maintain all collected data for five years, then archive securely to meet grant reporting requirements.
Store data for ten years, implementing tiered access controls to balance research utility and privacy.
Retain data indefinitely, establishing a governance framework to manage evolving legal and ethical standards over.
review: Balancing archival depth with regulatory compliance; options ignore the cost of long‑term data stewardship and the need for periodic audit.

name: Interdisciplinary Collaboration Framework
decision_axis: This lever controls cross‑disciplinary integration by choosing between siloed project teams, joint task forces, and shared governance councils.
consequences: Siloed teams allow focused expertise but risk misalignment of objectives. Joint task forces promote real‑time collaboration, enhancing innovation, yet require coordination overhead. Shared governance councils provide strategic alignment across disciplines, but may slow decision‑making due to broader representation.
options: Organize siloed project teams that focus exclusively on their domain expertise to streamline deliverables.
Establish joint task forces that meet weekly to coordinate data collection, analysis, and protocol adjustments.
Create a shared governance council that includes representatives from all core disciplines to set strategic priorities.
review: Tension between specialization and integration; options fail to address the challenge of aligning incentives across disciplines.

name: Funding Diversification Pathways
decision_axis: This lever controls funding sources by choosing between single grant reliance, diversified public‑private mix, and contingency reserve building.
consequences: Relying on a single grant simplifies budgeting but exposes the project to funding cuts. A diversified public‑private mix spreads risk and may unlock additional resources, yet introduces complex stakeholder expectations. Building a contingency reserve cushions against shortfalls but requires upfront capital allocation.
options: Maintain reliance on the primary DFG grant, allocating all budgetary planning around its timelines and and.
Pursue a diversified public‑private funding mix, securing supplemental support from university and industry partners for.
Establish a contingency reserve equal to 10% of total budget, funded through early cost‑overrun provisions.
review: Balancing financial stability with stakeholder complexity; options overlook the impact of funding mix on research independence.
